content:
Of course. Here are 5 multiple choice questions on Minimum Mean Square Error Forecasts and Their Properties, progressing in difficulty.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "q1",
    "question": "What is the primary goal of a Minimum Mean Square Error (MMSE) forecast?",
    "options": [
      "To minimize the average of the squared forecast errors",
      "To ensure forecasts are always unbiased",
      "To maximize the likelihood of the observed data",
      "To create forecasts that are always above the actual values"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The MMSE forecast is explicitly derived to minimize the expected value of the squared forecast error, E[(y_{t+l} - \hat{y}_{t+l})^2], which is the mean square error."
  },
  {
    "id": "q2",
    "question": "For a stationary AR(1) process, X_t = φ X_{t-1} + ε_t, what is the l-step ahead MMSE forecast?",
    "options": [
      "φ X_t",
      "φ^l X_t",
      "X_t",
      "0"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": "The MMSE forecast for an AR(1) model is derived by iterating the model forward. \hat{X}_t(l) = E[X_{t+l} | X_t, X_{t-1}, ...] = φ E[X_{t+l-1} | X_t, ...] = ... = φ^l X_t."
  },
  {
    "id": "q3",
    "question": "Which of these is a key property of the forecast errors from an MMSE forecast?",
    "options": [
      "They are always serially uncorrelated",
      "They form a white noise sequence",
      "Their variance decreases as the forecast horizon increases",
      "They have a mean of zero and are uncorrelated with each other"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 3,
    "explanation": "A fundamental property of MMSE forecast errors for lead time l is that they have a mean of zero and are uncorrelated with each other. For a given horizon l, they may be autocorrelated, but errors *at the same horizon* across time are not."
  },
  {
    "id": "q4",
    "question": "Why does the forecast variance increase as the forecast horizon (l) increases?",
    "options": [
      "Because the model parameters become less certain over time",
      "Because the cumulative effect of future random shocks (ε_t) becomes greater",
      "Because the assumption of stationarity weakens for distant forecasts",
      "Because the forecast error is always proportional to the square of the horizon"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": The forecast uncertainty is directly linked to the future white noise shocks (ε_{t+1}, ε_{t+2}, ..., ε_{t+l}) that cannot be predicted. As the horizon l increases, more of these unpredictable shocks accumulate, leading to greater variance in the forecast distribution."
  },
  {
    "id": "q5",
    "question": "For a process whose first difference is white noise, (1-B)Y_t = ε_t, what is the MMSE forecast for all lead times l ≥ 1?",
    "options": [
      "0",
      "Y_t",
      "Y_t - Y_{t-1}",
      "The mean of the series, μ"
    ],
    "correctIndex": 1,
    "explanation": "This is a random walk process. The best forecast for any future value is simply the last observed value, Y_t, because the expected value of all future shocks is zero. Formally, Y_{t+l} = Y_t + ε_{t+1} + ... + ε_{t+l}, so the conditional expectation is E[Y_{t+l} | Y_t, ...] = Y_t."
  }
]
```
